text: Plato's allegory of the cave, set in Book VII of the Republic, describes prisoners chained since birth in an underground chamber. They face a blank wall and can only see shadows cast by objects passing before a fire behind them. For these prisoners, the shadows are reality — they have no frame of reference for anything more. When one prisoner is freed and forced to turn toward the fire, the light is painful; he wants to look away. Plato uses this not merely as metaphor but as a theory of knowledge: our habitual objects of attention shape what we take to be real.
question: According to the passage, why do the chained prisoners mistake shadows for reality?

options.text: They are too fearful to turn toward the fire
They have been deceived by the people carrying objects
They have no frame of reference for anything beyond what they habitually see
The fire is too dim to illuminate the cave walls properly
